title: The Picard Group of a Cominuscule Richardson Variety
speaker: Anders Buch
abstract: A Richardson variety R in a cominuscule Grassmannian is defined by a skew diagram of boxes. If this diagram has several connected components, then R is a product of smaller Richardson varieties given by the components. I will show that the Picard group of R is a free abelian group of rank equal to the number of components in its skew diagram. In addition, the integration pairing identifies the Picard group with the dual of the first Chow group of R. The proof is based on a resolution of singularities that is interesting in itself.
tags: Workshop on Combinatorics of Enumerative Geometry

proof_stages.label: Cominuscule Richardson dictionary
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up cominuscule flag varieties through diagrammatic parametrizations of T-fixed points, defines Schubert and opposite Schubert varieties and their Richardson intersections, and recalls rationality and normality.

proof_stages.label: Picard group via the first Chow group
proof_stages.summary: The goal of computing line bundles on Richardson varieties is reframed by using the intersection pairing Pic(X) x A_1(X) -> Z, since the class group is not known whereas A_1 may be computable.

proof_stages.label: Skew-shape invariance and component reduction
proof_stages.summary: Richardson varieties in the cominuscule setting are encoded by skew diagrams whose long/short-box posets determine the isomorphism class, and disconnected skew diagrams factor so that both A_1 and Picard groups split as direct sums over connected components.

proof_stages.label: Connected-case theorem and T-curve generation
proof_stages.summary: For a connected nonempty skew diagram the first Chow group is asserted to be Z, while outside type E the Picard group is its dual, and equivariant Chow theory reduces A_1 to classes of T-stable curves.

proof_stages.label: Adjacent fixed points and triangle surfaces
proof_stages.summary: Adjacency of fixed points is defined through T-stable curves, then two lemmas provide the key local geometry: long adjacent intervals in cominuscule posets contain an intermediate adjacent fixed point, and three pairwise adjacent fixed points lie in a T-stable irreducible surface with A_1 isomorphic to Z.

proof_stages.label: Computation of A_1 in the connected case
proof_stages.summary: T-convexity of Richardson varieties puts the auxiliary surfaces inside the Richardson variety, so the three boundary T-curves have the same Chow class and every T-curve class can be reduced to the common class of a one-box Richardson curve.

proof_stages.label: Resolution comparison and final Picard computation
proof_stages.summary: A specially chosen resolution with perfect Picard--A_1 pairing gives, via normality and pullback of line bundles, injectivity of Pic(X) -> A_1(X)^*, while explicit line bundles give surjectivity, yielding Pic(X) as a free abelian group with one generator per connected skew component outside type E.

examples.name: Elliptic curve obstruction to Picard--Chow injectivity
examples.note: An elliptic curve is mentioned to show that the natural map from the Picard group to the dual of the first Chow group is not injective for arbitrary varieties, so injectivity must be proved in the Richardson setting.
examples.engagement_reason: He invokes an elliptic curve only to state that its Picard group is the curve while its first Chow group is Z, giving a non-injectivity obstruction without deriving it.

passes.reason: An elliptic curve is used to show the Picard-to-Chow-dual map can fail to be injective via its nontrivial Pic^0 kernel.
An ordinary elliptic curve is used to show the Picard-to-Chow-dual map can have nontrivial Pic^0 kernel, so injectivity fails.
the elliptic curve is used to show Picard-to-Chow-dual injectivity can fail, while elliptic curves form an ordinary genus-one family
An elliptic curve is used to show the Picard-to-Chow-dual map need not be injective.
an elliptic curve is used explicitly to show the Picard-to-Chow-dual map need not be injective
An ordinary elliptic curve is used to show the Picard-to-dual-Chow degree map can fail to be injective.
The elliptic curve is used to show the Picard-to-Chow-dual map need not be injective, but the curve itself is not a sporadic, degenerate, extremal, or maximally symmetric special object here.
An elliptic curve is used to show Picard-to-Chow-dual injectivity can fail, while structurally it is an ordinary smooth genus-one curve.
An ordinary elliptic curve is used to show the Picard-to-Chow-dual map need not be injective.
The elliptic curve is used to show Picard--Chow injectivity can fail, while the curve itself is structurally ordinary here.
used to show the Picard-to-Chow-dual map need not be injective, while an elliptic curve is a standard nondegenerate genus-one curve
Used to show Picard-to-Chow-dual injectivity can fail; a generic elliptic curve is not structurally sporadic or extremal.

examples.name: Cayley plane realized as a skew Richardson variety
examples.note: A skew shape in an exceptional cominuscule diagram is identified with the poset of the Cayley plane, illustrating that the Richardson isomorphism class depends only on the skew poset rather than on its ambient placement.
examples.engagement_reason: He applies the skew-shape/poset criterion to a specific skew Richardson shape and explains that, after flipping the poset, it realizes the E6/P6 Cayley plane.

passes.reason: The Cayley plane is the exceptional homogeneous variety E6/P, a highly symmetric cominuscule space, here used illustratively as a skew Richardson variety.
The Cayley plane is the exceptional homogeneous variety E6/P6 and is used illustratively as a skew Richardson variety, not adversarially.
the Cayley plane is the exceptional octonionic Hermitian symmetric space E6/P6, used here as a positive Richardson realization
The Cayley plane is the exceptional E6 cominuscule homogeneous variety, presented here as a Richardson example.
the Cayley plane is the exceptional E6/P6 cominuscule homogeneous symmetric variety, here used only to illustrate a skew Richardson realization
The Cayley plane is the exceptional E6/P6 cominuscule homogeneous variety, used illustratively as a skew Richardson variety.
The Cayley plane is the exceptional E6/P6 Hermitian-symmetric homogeneous variety, used illustratively as a Richardson realization rather than as a refutation.
The Cayley plane is presented as a Richardson-variety realization, and it is an exceptional type-E homogeneous variety rather than a classical-family member.
The Cayley plane is an exceptional E6 cominuscule homogeneous variety, presented as a skew Richardson variety rather than as an obstruction.
The Cayley plane is the exceptional E6 cominuscule homogeneous space, used as a Richardson realization rather than as a refutation.
the Cayley plane is the exceptional highly symmetric homogeneous space E6/P6, used as a positive Richardson-variety realization
Presented as a realization/example of the skew Richardson theorem; the Cayley plane is an exceptional E6/octonionic homogeneous variety.

examples.name: Adjacent fixed points in Gr(5,12)
examples.note: Two explicit coordinate fixed points in the Grassmannian are drawn as Young diagrams to make concrete the criterion that adjacency corresponds to a connected rim in the skew diagram.
examples.engagement_reason: He explicitly chooses two fixed points in Gr(5,12), writes their spanning basis vectors, draws the corresponding Young diagrams, and checks adjacency via the connected rim shape.

passes.reason: The adjacent Grassmannian fixed points form a minimal Bruhat-edge case, equivalently distinct 5-planes with maximal possible intersection dimension 4.
The two coordinate 5-planes intersect in dimension m-1, the closest possible for distinct Grassmannian fixed points, illustrating adjacency.
the two coordinate 5-planes are adjacent, with maximal possible 4-dimensional intersection among distinct 5-planes
The two fixed 5-planes are adjacent, i.e. at minimal possible separation among distinct fixed points.
the two Grassmannian fixed points are adjacent, i.e. distinct 5-planes with maximal possible intersection dimension 4 and joined by a minimal T-stable curve
The two coordinate 5-planes differ by one basis vector, so their intersection has the maximal possible dimension for distinct planes and they illustrate adjacency.
The two coordinate 5-planes are used to illustrate adjacency, and adjacency is the minimal-distance/maximal-intersection case for distinct Grassmannian fixed points.
The adjacent fixed points in Gr(5,12) illustrate the adjacency criterion and form a minimal nontrivial pair connected by a T-stable curve.
The two fixed 5-planes are adjacent, i.e. have maximal possible intersection for distinct fixed points, and serve as an illustrative example.
The Gr(5,12) coordinate fixed points are a standard adjacent pair used to illustrate the definition.
the two fixed 5-planes are adjacent, meaning they have maximal possible intersection among distinct 5-planes and minimal Grassmann distance
Used illustratively for adjacency; the two coordinate 5-planes have maximal proper intersection and hence minimal nonzero Grassmann distance.

examples.name: The surface omega is P^2 in the Grassmannian case
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker explains that the auxiliary T-stable surface in the Grassmannian case is always a projective plane, illustrating the surface lemma used to identify curve classes.
examples.engagement_reason: He constructs the Grassmannian omega as subspaces between an intersection and a sum, checks the dimension jump, and identifies it concretely as P^2.

passes.reason: In the Grassmannian case omega is the projective plane P^2, a maximally homogeneous regular surface used to prove the Chow-class comparison.
In the Grassmannian case omega is the standard projective plane P^2, a maximally regular homogeneous surface used to compare curve classes.
omega is identified with P^2, the maximally symmetric projective plane, to compare curve classes rather than refute a claim
In the Grassmannian case omega is the projective plane, a maximally symmetric homogeneous surface used constructively.
omega is identified with P^2, a maximally homogeneous projective plane used as the Grassmannian case of the lemma
In the Grassmannian case the auxiliary surface is the highly symmetric projective plane P^2, used to support the Chow-equivalence argument.
In the Grassmannian case the auxiliary surface omega is simply the projective plane P^2, a maximally symmetric surface used to prove the Chow-class comparison, not to disprove a claim.
The Grassmannian omega surface is used constructively in the lemma, and as P^2 it is the maximally regular projective plane.
In the Grassmannian case omega is the highly symmetric projective plane P^2, used constructively in the lemma.
The surface omega is P^2, a maximally symmetric projective plane used to identify curve classes.
omega is identified in the Grassmannian case with P^2, a maximally homogeneous projective surface used to illustrate the lemma
Used to describe the Grassmannian form of omega; P^2 is the maximally regular projective plane.

examples.name: Non-cominuscule isotropic two-plane counterexample
examples.note: In the questions, an isotropic two-plane Grassmannian in a six-dimensional symplectic vector space is cited as a case where a Richardson variety has six fixed points and nine T-stable lines but no useful triangles, illustrating why the cominuscule hypothesis is needed.
examples.engagement_reason: He analyzes the isotropic two-plane Richardson counterexample by describing its fixed points and fixed-line graph and using the absence of triangles to show the omega-lemma gives no information.

passes.reason: The isotropic two-plane example is a classical non-cominuscule flag case whose Richardson subvariety lacks the needed T-stable triangles, so it refutes the lemma’s extension.
The classical non-cominuscule isotropic two-plane Richardson example is deployed to show the cominuscule adjacency/triangle lemma fails outside the cominuscule setting.
the non-cominuscule isotropic two-plane Richardson variety is presented as a counterexample to extending the cominuscule adjacency lemma
The non-cominuscule isotropic two-plane Richardson variety is cited to show the cominuscule adjacency lemma fails to give triangles.
the isotropic two-plane Richardson example in type C3 is used as a non-cominuscule case where the adjacency lemma/triangle argument fails
The symplectic isotropic two-plane Grassmannian example is deployed as a non-cominuscule case where the adjacency lemma or triangle method fails.
The non-cominuscule isotropic two-plane example is invoked to show the cominuscule adjacency/triangle argument fails, while the variety is not structurally sporadic or extremal.
The isotropic two-plane Richardson example is invoked to show the cominuscule adjacency lemma does not extend to this non-cominuscule setting.
The non-cominuscule isotropic two-plane Richardson example is used to show the adjacency/triangle argument fails outside the cominuscule setting.
The non-cominuscule symplectic isotropic two-plane example is cited as a failure of the cominuscule adjacency argument.
the isotropic two-plane Richardson example is given as a non-cominuscule failure case for the adjacency-triangle lemma
Used as a non-cominuscule case where the adjacency/triangle lemma strategy fails; IG(2,6) is a standard member of a systematic symplectic Grassmannian family.
